title: What Is LlamaIndex? The RAG Data Framework
seoTitle: LlamaIndex Explained: The RAG Data Framework
metaDescription: LlamaIndex is the data framework for connecting LLMs to your own documents. Learn how it handles ingestion, indexing, and retrieval for RAG apps.
oneLiner: You will understand what LlamaIndex is, the ingestion-to-retrieval pipeline it provides, and when to reach for it to build a RAG app over your own data.

blocks.md: A [large language model](/learn/llm-fundamentals/llm-basics/what-is-an-llm) is brilliant but boxed in: it only knows what it learned during training, and it has never seen *your* PDFs, your wiki, or your support tickets. To answer questions from your own documents, you need to do four jobs every time — load the files, split them up, find the relevant pieces for a question, and hand those pieces to the model. **LlamaIndex** is a framework that does all four jobs for you.

Think of LlamaIndex as a research librarian for your AI. You give the librarian a pile of documents once. They read everything, file it into an organized index, and remember where each fact lives. Later, when someone asks a question, the librarian doesn't re-read the whole pile — they walk straight to the right shelf, pull the three relevant pages, and pass them to the analyst (the LLM) who writes the answer. LlamaIndex is the plumbing that makes that librarian exist.

In one line: LlamaIndex is a **data framework for [RAG](/learn/rag/rag-fundamentals/what-is-rag)**. It connects an LLM to your private data by handling the unglamorous middle — ingestion, indexing, and retrieval — so you can ask questions over documents the model was never trained on, and get grounded answers back.

The name is a clue. An *index* is the structure a library uses to find things fast. LlamaIndex builds and queries that index for your documents — it is not a model and not a chatbot, it is the layer that gets the right text in front of whatever model you choose.

You *can* build RAG by hand — read files, call an embedding API, store vectors in a database, run a similarity search, and stitch the results into a prompt. People do it, and a tiny version fits in a few dozen lines. The problem is that a real app is never the tiny version.

items: **Many file types.** Real knowledge lives in PDFs, Word docs, HTML, Notion pages, Slack, databases, and APIs — each with its own quirks. Writing a loader for every one is tedious and easy to get wrong.
**Chunking is fiddly.** Splitting documents into the right-sized passages, with sensible overlap and metadata, quietly sets your quality ceiling. Doing it well by hand for every format is real work.

**Swappable parts.** You will want to try a different embedding model, a different vector store, or a reranker without rewriting everything. A framework gives you one interface and lets you change the pieces behind it.
**Retrieval is more than top-k.** Production systems need metadata filters, hybrid search, reranking, and ways to combine results from several indexes. That logic adds up fast.

blocks.md: LlamaIndex exists so you don't rebuild this pipeline from scratch for every project. It ships a large set of **data connectors** (often called LlamaHub), battle-tested chunking, a clean abstraction over dozens of vector stores and embedding providers, and retrieval logic you can compose. You write a few lines describing *what* you want; the framework handles *how*.

Who should care? Anyone building "chat with your documents," an internal knowledge assistant, a documentation search bot, or an [agent](/learn/ai-agents/agent-fundamentals/what-is-an-ai-agent) that needs to look things up. If your hardest problem is *connecting an LLM to your data* rather than orchestrating a complex multi-step workflow, LlamaIndex is built squarely for you — that data focus is what sets it apart from more general toolkits like [LangChain](/learn/agent-frameworks/langchain-ecosystem/what-is-langchain).

LlamaIndex organizes everything around five core ideas. Learn these five words and the whole framework clicks into place: **documents**, **nodes**, **indexes**, **retrievers**, and **query engines**. Data flows through them left to right.

A **Document** is one source file LlamaIndex has loaded — a PDF, a web page, a database row. You get them with a *data connector* (a reader). Because a 200-page manual is far too big to search or prompt with as one unit, LlamaIndex splits each Document into **Nodes**: bite-sized chunks of text. A Node is the real unit of retrieval. Crucially, each Node carries **metadata** (source file, page number, author, date) and links back to its neighbors, so a retrieved chunk can be traced to its origin and cited.

An **Index** is the searchable structure built over your Nodes. The most common type is the **vector index**: every Node is turned into an [embedding](/learn/embeddings-vector-databases/embeddings-explained/what-are-embeddings) — a list of numbers capturing its meaning — and stored in a [vector database](/learn/embeddings-vector-databases/vector-database-guides/what-is-a-vector-database) so similar chunks sit close together. LlamaIndex also offers other index shapes (summary, keyword, tree, knowledge-graph) for different query patterns, but the vector index is the workhorse of most RAG apps.

A **Retriever** is the component that, given a question, returns the most relevant Nodes from an Index — usually by embedding the question and finding the closest chunks ([semantic search](/learn/embeddings-vector-databases/similarity-search-indexing/what-is-semantic-search)). A **Query Engine** wraps the whole round trip: it takes a natural-language question, calls the retriever, assembles the chunks into a prompt, sends it to the LLM, and returns the synthesized answer — along with the source Nodes it used. That last part is the payoff of all the metadata: you get an answer *and* its citations.
In practice the simplest path is three lines: load a folder, build a vector index, ask a question. Everything above is happening under the hood.

blocks.title: minimal LlamaIndex RAG
blocks.code:
from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, SimpleDirectoryReader

# 1) LOAD: a connector reads every file in the folder into Documents.
documents = SimpleDirectoryReader("./my_docs").load_data()

# 2) INDEX: split into Nodes, embed them, store the vectors.
#    (chunking + embedding + storage all happen here)
index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(documents)

# 3) QUERY: a query engine retrieves the best Nodes,
#    then asks the LLM to answer using only that context.
query_engine = index.as_query_engine()
response = query_engine.query("What is our refund window for physical items?")

print(response)                 # the synthesized answer
print(response.source_nodes)    # the chunks it used -> your citations

blocks.md: Notice what you *didn't* write: no chunking loop, no embedding calls, no vector-store client, no prompt template. That is the entire point of a data framework — the four RAG steps are still happening, but LlamaIndex assembles them so you describe intent, not plumbing.

sections.title: The building blocks at a glance

blocks.md: Here is the same vocabulary as a quick reference. When you read LlamaIndex docs or tutorials, these are the nouns that show up again and again.

rows: Raw input — one PDF, page, or record
A chunk of a Document + metadata
The unit that gets embedded and retrieved
Organizes Nodes so search is fast
Lets the index match on meaning, not keywords
Returns the top Nodes for a query
Turns a question into a grounded answer

blocks.md: One step up from a single query engine is a **chat engine** (which keeps conversation history) and an **agent** (which can call query engines as tools and decide *when* to look something up). But every one of those is built from the same blocks in the table. Master the table and you understand the framework.

The most common question beginners ask is how LlamaIndex differs from [LangChain](/learn/agent-frameworks/langchain-ecosystem/what-is-langchain). They overlap a lot and both can build RAG, so the honest answer is about *emphasis*, not a hard wall.

columns.title: LlamaIndex
items: Built first for data: ingest, index, retrieve
Rich index types and retrieval strategies
RAG is the default mental model

columns.title: LangChain
items: Built first for chaining LLM steps
RAG is one capability among many

blocks.md: A useful rule of thumb: if your core challenge is *getting the right text out of a large, messy pile of documents*, start with LlamaIndex. If your core challenge is *orchestrating a complex chain of steps, tools, and decisions*, lean toward LangChain or a graph-based framework. In reality many teams use both — and LlamaIndex retrievers can plug into LangChain workflows — so this is a starting point, not a religion. For a deeper side-by-side, see [LlamaIndex vs LangChain](/learn/agent-frameworks/orchestration-frameworks/llamaindex-vs-langchain).

Both frameworks evolve quickly and their feature sets keep converging. Pick based on which mental model fits your problem today and which docs you find clearer, not on a feature checklist that will be stale next quarter.
A framework removes the boilerplate, but it cannot make bad inputs good. Most LlamaIndex disappointments are really RAG problems wearing a framework costume.

items: **Treating it as magic.** The three-line demo is real, but it uses default chunk sizes, the default embedding model, and an in-memory store. That is great for a prototype and weak for production. Know which defaults you are accepting.

**Ignoring chunking.** If retrieval returns the wrong passages, the answer is wrong no matter how good the model is. Chunk size, overlap, and metadata are tunable in LlamaIndex for a reason — adjust them when quality lags.
**Forgetting persistence.** The quick demo rebuilds the index in memory every run, re-embedding everything. For anything real, persist the index to a vector store so you embed once and reload fast.
**Skipping reranking and filters.** Plain top-k vector search is the floor, not the ceiling. LlamaIndex supports metadata filters and rerankers; production retrieval usually needs them.

**No evaluation.** "It answered three questions well" is not a test. Measure whether retrieval surfaces the right Nodes and whether answers stay faithful to them before you trust it — see [why RAG still hallucinates](/learn/rag/rag-fundamentals/why-rag-hallucinates).

blocks.md: Retrieved documents are untrusted input. Text pulled from a web page or a shared file can carry hidden instructions aimed at the model — [prompt injection](/learn/prompt-engineering/prompt-injection-security/what-is-prompt-injection). A framework will happily retrieve and inject that text, so treat anything indexed as data, never as commands.
Once the five building blocks make sense, the rest of LlamaIndex is mostly *better versions of each stage*. A few directions worth knowing as you grow past the basics.

**Better ingestion.** Real documents are messy — tables, scanned pages, mixed layouts. LlamaIndex offers an ingestion pipeline with caching and document management (so changed files re-index without redoing everything), plus advanced parsing for complex PDFs. Good Nodes in means good answers out; this stage repays the most effort.

**Advanced retrieval.** Beyond top-k vector search you can layer metadata filtering, hybrid (keyword + semantic) search, **reranking** with a second model that re-scores candidates, and query transformations that rewrite or decompose a messy question before retrieval. LlamaIndex exposes these as composable retriever and post-processor components, so you upgrade one stage without touching the rest.

**Routers and multiple indexes.** When your knowledge spans very different sources — say a code wiki and a legal handbook — you can build one index per source and put a **router** in front that sends each question to the right index, or queries several and merges the results. This is how a single assistant answers across many domains without one giant, noisy index.

**Agents and tools.** A query engine can be wrapped as a tool an [agent](/learn/ai-agents/agent-fundamentals/what-is-an-ai-agent) calls on demand, turning a fixed retrieve-then-answer flow into a loop where the model decides *whether* and *what* to look up — the same idea as [agentic RAG](/learn/rag/advanced-rag/what-is-agentic-rag). LlamaIndex also supports structured outputs and workflows for multi-step tasks, edging into territory it shares with general orchestration frameworks.

The durable lesson is the same one that governs all RAG: a LlamaIndex app is only as good as the Nodes its retriever puts in front of the model. The framework saves you enormous plumbing work and gives you clean places to improve each stage — but your effort still belongs mostly in ingestion and retrieval quality, not in prompt wording. Start with the three-line version, measure where it falls short, and upgrade one block at a time.

faq.a: LlamaIndex is a data framework for building RAG applications — connecting a large language model to your own documents. It handles loading files, splitting them into chunks, indexing them for fast search, retrieving the relevant pieces for a question, and feeding them to the model. It is the standard choice for 'chat with your documents' and internal knowledge-assistant apps.

Neither is strictly better — they emphasize different things. LlamaIndex is built first around data: ingestion, indexing, and retrieval, so it shines when your hardest problem is getting the right text out of a large document pile. LangChain is built first around orchestrating multi-step LLM workflows and agents. Many teams use both, since LlamaIndex retrievers can plug into LangChain pipelines.

A Node is a chunk of a Document plus its metadata (source, page, and links to neighboring chunks). LlamaIndex splits each loaded Document into Nodes because a whole file is too big to search or prompt with directly. Nodes are the actual unit that gets embedded, indexed, and retrieved, and their metadata is what lets answers cite their sources.

faq.q: Do I need a vector database to use LlamaIndex?
faq.a: Not to start. By default LlamaIndex can build a simple in-memory index, which is perfect for prototypes. For anything real you should plug in a vector store (such as a hosted or self-hosted vector database) so you embed your documents once, persist the index, and reload it quickly instead of rebuilding every run.

faq.q: Does LlamaIndex work with any LLM?
faq.a: Yes. LlamaIndex is model-agnostic: it provides one interface over many LLM and embedding providers, so you can choose your model and swap it later without rewriting your pipeline. It focuses on the data layer — ingestion, indexing, and retrieval — and treats the LLM as a pluggable component that generates the final answer.

faq.q: Can LlamaIndex build agents, not just RAG?
faq.a: Yes. You can wrap a query engine as a tool that an agent calls on demand, letting the model decide whether and what to look up rather than always retrieving first. LlamaIndex also supports chat engines, structured outputs, and workflows for multi-step tasks, though heavy agent orchestration is where it overlaps with more general frameworks.
